coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon dots modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres via in situ calcination of cyanamide and glucose for highly enhanced visible light photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Yuan
Lin, Zhi
Deng, Jiawei
Liu, Yingliang
Zhang, Li
Wang, Kunlun
Xu, Shengang
Cao, Shaokui
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a series of carbon dots (CDs) modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres (HCNS-Cx) were constructed via a double in situ approach using cyanamide and glucose as precursors, respectively. As HCNS-Cx was synthesized by one-step in situ thermal polymerization of two precursors, which could make CDs and g-C3N4 keep tight connection and increase the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The average diameter and wall thickness of the HCNS-Cx are about 355 nm and 55 nm, respectively. Under the visible light irradiation, the H2 evolution rate (HER) of HCNS-C1.0 (2322 μmol g−1 h−1) was 19 times that of bulk g-C3N4 (122 μmol g−1 h−1) and 1.8 times that of HCNS without CDs modification (1289 μmol g−1 h−1), respectively. And its apparent quantum efficiency is 17.93% at 420 nm. The specific surface area, light absorption capacity, and charge carrier mobility of HCNS-Cx could be dramatically improved due to the introduction of CDs and hollow structures of g-C3N4 spheres, resulting in a significant improvement of photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Carbon dots
Hollow spheres
In situ calcination
Photocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a, b) HCNS. TEM images of (c) BCN, (d) HCNS and (e, f) HCNS–C1.0.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of BCN, HCNS and HCNS-Cx.
High-resolution XPS spectra of BCN, HCNS and HCNS–C1.0: (a) C 1s and (b) N 1s.
(a) UV–Vis and (b) PL spectra of BCN, HCNS and HCNS-Cx.

(a) Reaction time profile of the H2 evolution reaction; (b) Photocatalytic H2 evolution of as-prepared samples; (c) Time course of H2 evolution for HCNS–C1.0; (d) Photocurrent response, (e) EIS and (f) Band-gap structures of BCN, HCNS and HCNS–C1.0.
The possible photocatalytic mechanism for the H2 evolution of HCNS–C1.0.
Schematic diagram of the preparation of HCNS-Cx composite.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon dots modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres via in situ calcination of cyanamide and glucose for highly enhanced visible light photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Advanced Nylon Materials and Application, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Advanced Nylon Materials and Application
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Advanced Nylon Materials and Application, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a series of carbon dots (CDs) modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres (HCNS-Cx) were constructed via a double in situ approach using cyanamide and glucose as precursors, respectively. As HCNS-Cx was synthesized by one-step in situ thermal polymerization of two precursors, which could make CDs and g-C3N4 keep tight connection and increase the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The average diameter and wall thickness of the HCNS-Cx are about 355 nm and 55 nm, respectively. Under the visible light irradiation, the H2 evolution rate (HER) of HCNS-C1.0 (2322 μmol g−1 h−1) was 19 times that of bulk g-C3N4 (122 μmol g−1 h−1) and 1.8 times that of HCNS without CDs modification (1289 μmol g−1 h−1), respectively. And its apparent quantum efficiency is 17.93% at 420 nm. The specific surface area, light absorption capacity, and charge carrier mobility of HCNS-Cx could be dramatically improved due to the introduction of CDs and hollow structures of g-C3N4 spheres, resulting in a significant improvement of photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free polymeric semiconductor, has attracted enormous attention for application in water splitting [1,2], pollutant degradation [3,4] and CO2 reduction [5,6] on account of its visible light response, low cost, and reliable stability [7–10] Although g-C3N4 is considered to be an excellent visible light photocatalyst, the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, low surface area and narrow absorption of sunlight limit its photocatalytic activity [8]. In order to solve these problems, several modification methods such as morphology control (nanoplates, nanotubes and hollow sphere nanostructures) [11–14], doping (C, N and O) [15,16], copolymerization [17,18] and heterojunction construction (ZnO, TiO2 and BiVO4) [19,20], have been developed.

Carbon Dots (CDs), as a new zero-dimensional carbon material, have been frequently used in photocatalysts, electrocatalysts and biosensors because of their unique properties [21–23]. Recently, it has been reported that CDs coupled with some photocatalysts, such as TiO2 [24,25], CdS [26,27] and BiVO4 [28], can improve their visible light absorption and reduce the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. In addition, the modification of g-C3N4 with CDs also showed satisfactory results because the π-π interaction could form between g-C3N4 and CDs [17,27,29]. Zhang et al. [18] reported that the CDs/g-C3N4 composites, which was prepared by calcinating the mixture of CDs ad g-C3N4 at 300 °C, exhibited excellent phenol degradation ability. Meanwhile, as an advanced material preparation method, in situ growth method works better than the direct combination of CDs and g-C3N4 [30]. For example, Wang et al. [31] fabricated CDs/g-C3N4 (10%) photocatalyst via hydrothermal treatment of L-ascorbic acid and g-C3N4. In this CDs/g-C3N4 composite, the CDs formed by in situ growth of L-ascorbic acid on g-C3N4 have satisfying dispersibility and maintain a tight connection with g-C3N4. Under UV light irradiation, the HER of CDs/g-C3N4 (10%) is 2.2 times higher than that of BCN. Based on the similar strategy, Fang et al. [32] constructed CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst using CDs and dicyandiamide. The photocatalyst also showed excellent photocatalytic performance. Previously, our group has proposed a novel strategy for the preparation of CDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst [33]. The photocatalyst was constructed by one-step double in situ approach via the concurrent thermal polymerization of urea and glucose, its HER is 4.4 times higher than that of BCN.

At the same time, some studies showed that the hollow sphere structure is beneficial to increase multiple reflection and scattering of light, thus improving the light absorption capacity [34]. In addition, the specific surface area of photocatalyst also increased owing to the existence of a hollow structure [35,36]. Wang et al. [37] reported a simple hard-templating approach to synthesizing polymeric hollow nanospheres of graphitic carbon nitride (HCNS). The HCNS advantages from the inner optical reflection and improved structure condensation provide a remarkably increased photochemical activity, reaching an overall AQY of approximately 7.5% and the HER for Pt/HCNS-1 was 224 μmol h−1. Luo et al. [38] prepared a graphitic carbon integrated graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) hollow sphere via the modified shape-selective templating method. Firstly, graphitic carbon species were embedded into MCM-41 by calcination. Then the melamine and carbon modified MCM-41 were calcined at 550 °C to prepare the graphitic carbon modified g-C3N4 vesicles. The advantageous vesicle structure enhanced light absorption via the light scattering effect, while further carbon modification provided an efficient pathway to promote charge separation and transfer. The resultant materials achieved excellent photocatalytic performance with their HER reaching 718.1 μmol g−1 h−1, approximately 15 times higher than that of the bulk g-C3N4.

Herein, we report the in situ fabrication of CDs modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres (HCNS-Cx, x = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0) by the concurrent calcination of their precursors, cyanamide and glucose. Firstly, both cyanamide and glucose were absorbed on the surface of SiO2/mSiO2 template. Then, the above composite was calcined at 550 °C to form CDs modified g-C3N4 spheres. Finally, SiO2 was etched by NH4HF2 to form CDs modified g-C3N4 spheres (HCNS-Cx, x = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0). As the one-step in situ thermal polymerization of two precursors could make CDs and g-C3N4 keep a tight connection, causing the improvement of the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and charge transporting ability of HCNS-Cx. The specific surface area and light absorption of HCNS-Cx increased significantly in the presence of hollow sphere structure, resulting in the improvement of photocatalytic activity. Among them, HCNS-C1.0 exhibited the highest HER, which was about 19 times that of the bulk g-C3N4 and 1.8 times that of HCNS without CDs modification, respectively. Considering CDs modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres via one-step in situ approach are effective ways to improve photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4-based nanocomposite, the combination of one step in situ approach and hard template may extend to other CDs modified hollow semiconductor nanocomposites such as TiO2, CeO2 and Cu2O.

Glucose and ammonia solution (32 wt%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99%), trimethoxy (octadecyl) silane (C18TMOS, 90%), ammonium bifluoride (98%), chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O, Pt > 37.5%) and cyanamide (50% aqueous solution with 1% formic acid stabilizer) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Shanghai, China. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

Bulk g-C3N4 was prepared via thermal condensation using cyanamide as the precursor. Typically, cyanamide (1.0 g) was put into a crucible and sealed with aluminum foil. Then the crucible was transferred to a tube furnace, heated to 550 °C and kept for 4 h under Ar atmosphere. The obtained material was named as BCN.

The SiO2/mSiO2 template was prepared via three steps according to the previous literature [35]. Firstly, SiO2 spheres were synthesized by Stöber method. In detail, the aqueous ammonia (32 wt%, 4.35 mL) was mixed with deionized water (10 mL) and ethanol (74 mL). After the mixture was stirred for 30 min, TEOS (5.6 mL) was added to the above-mixed solution. Then the mixture was kept stationary for 1 h to form a uniform imporous SiO2 sphere. Secondly, SiO2 spheres with a thin layer of mesoporous SiO2 were formed. Specifically, a mixture of TEOS (5.4 mL) and C18TMOS (2.6 mL) was added dropwise into the above solution under magnetic stirring. The mixed solution was kept for 3 h without stirring, and a thin layer of mesoporous SiO2 was wrapped around the core of SiO2 microspheres. The product was centrifuged, washed and dried at 70 °C for 4 h to obtain a white solid. Finally, the SiO2/mSiO2 template was obtained by calcinating the white solid at 550 °C for 6 h in air.

The CDs modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres were prepared by double in situ approach using cyanamide and glucose as precursors, and SiO2/mSiO2 as hard template, respectively. The schematic diagram is shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, cyanamide (10 g) and different contents of glucose (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mg) were dissolved in deionized water. Then, SiO2/mSiO2 template (2 g) was added into above solution, stirred under vacuum for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixtures were dispersed under ultrasonic waves for 3 h, and then stirred overnight. The white powder was obtained by centrifugation and dried at 60 °C. Then, the white powder was calcined under Ar atmosphere at 550 °C for 4 h to obtain SiO2/g-C3N4/CDs hybrids. Finally, the hybrids were treated with NH4HF2 (4 mol/L) for 12 h to remove SiO2 template. After centrifugation, washing and drying, a series of CDs modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres were obtained and named as HCNS-Cx (x = 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0). For comparison, hollow g-C3N4 spheres without CDs was also prepared in the same procedure and named as HCNS.

The morphology and microstructure of the samples were determined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta 250 FEG) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TalosF200S). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were characterized on a diffractometer (Rigaku Ultima IV) with Cu-Kα(λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation. Fourier trans-formed infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on an infrared spectrometer (Bruker Tensor II). UV-Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-Vis DRS) were obtained from a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 5000). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were characterized at room temperature on a fluorescent spectrophotometer (QuantaMaster 40). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250 Xi) with Al-Kα source (E = 1486.6 eV).

Photoelectrochemical characterization was carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, CHI) with a standard three-electrode system. The platinum wire and a saturated Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. For the working electrodes, 2 mg of the as-prepared composites were thoroughly dispersed in 0.5 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide. Then the suspension was dropped onto FTO glass and dried at 80 °C in a drying oven. 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte in this system. The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp with an ultraviolet cut off filter (λ > 420 nm). The test of photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted at a −0.2 V bias potential.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution experiments were carried out in a fully automated photocatalytic system (Labsolar-III AI, Perfectlight), and a 300 W Xe lamp with an ultraviolet cut off filter (λ > 420 nm) was used as a light source. In detail, 50 mg of the photocatalysts were dispersed into 100 mL aqueous solution containing 10 vol% triethanolamine, and 3 wt% Pt was in situ photodeposited on the photocatalysts as co-catalyst using H2PtCl6·6H2O as the precursor. The generated H2 was analyzed by using gas chromatograph (GC7900, TianMei) with a TCD detector and a capillary column (5 Å molecular sieve). High purity nitrogen was used as the carrier of the gas chromatography. During the reaction, the temperature of the solution was controlled at 7 °C by a flow of cooling water.

The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for H2 evolution at the wavelength of 420 nm and 450 nm was evaluated under the same photocatalytic reaction condition by placing various band-pass filters in front of the light source. The spectroradiometer (Avantes AvaSpec-2048-USB2, Netherlands) was used to determine the intensity of the incident light. The AQE value was obtained according to the following Equation [39]:
AQE
( % )
=
N u m b e r
o f
r e a c t e d
e l e c t r o n s
N u m b e r

i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
× 100
%
=
N u m b e r
o f
e v o l v e d
H 2
m o l e c u l e s
×
2
N u m b e r
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
× 100
%

The morphologies and microstructures of SiO2, SiO2/mSiO2, BCN, HCNS and HCNS-C1.0 were investigated by SEM and TEM. As displayed in Fig. S1, the prepared SiO2 and SiO2/mSiO2 show spherical structure. As seen from Fig. 1 b, HCNS exhibits a hollow spherical structure with diameters ranging from 312 nm to 363 nm. The TEM image of BCN is presented in Fig. 1c, where a typical two-dimensional accumulated layered structure of g-C3N4 is observed [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 1d, it can be seen that HCNS has an obvious hollow sphere structure with wall thickness of 50–60 nm. At the same time, there are a few damaged grains around the hollow sphere, mainly caused by the damage of HCNS in the process of template-etching and freeze-drying [38]. In Fig. 1e, the structure of HCNS-C1.0 still maintains a hollow sphere structure with a wall thickness of 53–60 nm, indicating that the introduction of CDs does not change the morphology of the HCNS. As shown in Fig. 1f, CDs are closely connected with HCNS without obvious agglomeration, and the size of CDs is about 11 nm. These results suggest that CDs modified HCNS were successfully constructed by combining a double in situ approach and template-based method.

The phase and chemical structures of BCN, HCNS and HCNS-Cx were characterized by XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the XRD patterns show that HCNS-Cx retains similar diffraction peak positions as BCN, but the shape of peaks have slightly changed. The stronger diffraction peak at 27.3° could be attributed to the stacking of the 2D conjugated aromatic system and indexed as (002) peak. The weaker peak at 13.3° could be assigned to the in-plane tri-s-triazine repeating units and indexed as (100) peak [17,42,43]. Compared with BCN, the peak at 27.3° of HCNS became much broader, implying that the formation of hollow sphere structures [43,44]. The presence of hollow sphere structures can reduce the crystallinity of g-C3N4, resulting in a broader XRD peak. The characteristic peaks of CDs could not be observed in the XRD patterns of HCNS-Cx because the content of CDs was below the detectable limits of XRD instrument [45]. The result of XRD pattern indicates that the integration of a small amount of CDs will not significantly change the phase structure of HCNS. Furthermore, FT-IR measurement is shown in Fig. 2b, the typical characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 can be observed in all samples [46]. Specifically, the peak at 800 cm−1 is considered as the stretching vibration of triazine cycles. The peaks at 1200-1700 cm−1 belong to the typical stretching vibration of C–N and CN, and the peaks at 3000-3500 cm−1 can be ascribed to the terminal amino group (–NH2 or –NH–) [46,47]. Compared with BCN, the peaks of HCNS-Cx at 3000-3500 cm−1 become broader because of the presence of uncondensed surface and residual N–H group and absorbed H2O [48,49]. A new peak that appeared at 2190 cm−1 can be ascribed to the CO2 adsorbed in the cavities of HCNS-Cx [50]. In addition, all the spectra of HCNS-Cx are similar to HCNS, implying that the introduction of CDs has not changed their chemical structures.

To study the surface chemical compositions of the constituent elements in HCNS-C1.0 composites, XPS spectra are further performed. Fig. 3 a, b exhibit the carbon (C 1s) and nitrogen (N 1s) elements that existed on the BCN, HCNS and HCNS-C1.0. For C 1s spectra (Fig. 3a), BCN displays three deconvolution peaks: N–CN (288.2 eV), C–NH2 (286.3 eV) and sp2 C–C bonds (284.6 eV) [51]. Besides, compared with the spectrum of BCN, the peaks of HCNS showed a slight blue shift. This phenomenon could be attributed to the fact that the hollow sphere structure reduces the polymerization degree of g-C3N4 and increases the amount of C–NH2, resulting in an increase in the peak area at 285.8 eV [52]. After the introduction of CDs, a new peak at 288.9 eV attributed to the formation of carboxyl groups (–COOH) on the surface of CDs appears, suggesting that CDs had distributed on the surface of the HCNS [53]. As observed from high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 3b), BCN can be deconvoluted into four peaks centered at 398.6 eV, 400.1 eV, 401.2 eV and 404.4 eV, which could be assigned to the sp2-hybridized nitrogen in aromatic triazine rings (CN–C), the tertiary nitrogen (N-(C)3), amino nitrogen (N–H) and positively charged g-C3N4 heterocycles, respectively [54]. Similar results are observed in the spectrum of HCNS. Comparatively, the C 1s and N 1s peaks in the XPS spectrum of HCNS-C1.0 are slightly shifted, showing that there is a coupling interaction between CDs and HCNS [40,55]. These interactions could lead to a high-quality contact interface and further promote the transportation of photogenerated carriers.

To study the influence of the introduction of CDs on the photophysical properties of g-C3N4, UV-Vis DRS and PL spectra of the as-prepared samples were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the optical absorption edge of HCNS exhibits a slightly blue-shift compared to BCN, which is consistent with the color change from yellow to pale-yellow (Fig. S2a–b) [56]. Furthermore, the absorption of HCNS in the visible region (λ ≥ 420 nm) is enhanced on account of the multiple reflection and scattering of light within the hollow sphere structure [57]. With the CDs content increase in the HCNS-Cx samples, the colors of composites change from pale yellow to brown (Fig. S2b–f), and the light absorption capacity of the HCNS-Cx increased. The enhanced visible light-harvesting could be ascribed to the introduction of CDs [58]. When CDs were combined with HCNS, the light absorption range of HCNS-Cx could extend to 800 nm, and the photocatalytic performance can be improved. As shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1, the band-gap energy (Eg) of BCN, HCNS, HCNS-Cx were calculated according to Tauc plots [59]. The Eg of BCN, HCNS, HCNS-C1.0 are estimated to be 2.79, 2.90 and 2.70 eV, respectively. Compared with BCN, the Eg of HCNS increase about 0.11 eV. The enlarged Eg of HCNS is related to the nanocrystalline properties of the hollow sphere structures, which also causes the blue-shift of the optical absorption edge [52]. In addition, the increased Eg of HCNS could result in the enhanced redox ability of photocarriers [60]. While CDs were introduced into HCNS, the Eg of HCNS-Cx narrowed to 2.70 eV, which is beneficial for the absorption of the sunlight.

As displayed in Fig. 4b, the strong PL emission peak is observed for the BCN due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Compared with BCN, HCNS exhibits reduced PL intensity, indicating that the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs decreased in HCNS. This may be owing to the porous structure of HCNS, which helps to reduce the migration path of photogenerated carriers and improve the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs in the photocatalyst [61,56]. As the content of CDs in HCNS-Cx increases, the PL intensity is significantly reduced, suggesting that the charge carrier recombination occurring in photocatalyst could be suppressed by the introduction of CDs on the surface of g-C3N4 [62,63]. This phenomenon can be explained that the electron-collecting ability of CDs can promote the migration of electrons generated by HCNS to CDs, thereby improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the photocatalyst [45]. The above results indicate that the introduction of CDs into HCNS via a double in situ approach can significantly improve the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs in HCNS-Cx photocatalysts.

The photocatalytic activities of all samples were evaluated with H2 evolution from the triethanolamine solution (TEOA) under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). As shown in Fig. 5 a, HCNS exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic HER (1289 μmol g−1 h−1), which is 10.6 times that of BCN (122 μmol g−1 h−1). As shown in Fig. S4 and Table S2, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of BCN and HCNS were 7.9 m2 g−1 and 42.01 m2 g−1, respectively, indicating that the hollow structure of HCNS could strongly increase the specific surface, hence, leading to the increase of active sites. Meanwhile, as the hollow spheres could improve the reflection and scattering of light in the internal cavity, the light utilization efficiency of the photocatalyst was also boosted [64]. Therefore, the HER of HCNS is obviously increased. In addition, all HCNS-Cx exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than that of HCNS. At the same time, the photocatalytic activities of HCNS-Cx increased first and then decreased with the variation of CDs contents. The highest HER (2322 μmol g−1 h−1) was observed for HCNS-C1.0, which is 1.8 times that of HCNS and 19 times that of BCN (122 μmol g−1 h−1), respectively. The HER of HCNS-C1.0 is much higher than that (4.4 times that of BCN) of our previous reported CDs/g-C3N4, which was constructed by one-step double in situ approach via the concurrent thermal polymerization of urea and glucose [33], demonstrating the hollow structure could dramatically improve the photocatalytic ability of CDs/g-C3N4 composite. Meanwhile, the HER of HCNS-C1.0 is also higher than that (15 times that of BCN) of a graphitic carbon integrated g-C3N4 hollow sphere by single in situ growth method [38], suggesting one-step double in situ method could further improve the photocatalytic activity of HCNS-C1.0. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of HCNS-C1.0 was calculated around 17.9% at 420 nm and 5.6% at 450 nm, respectively (Table S3). In addition, the comparison of photocatalytic activity with some previous g-C3N4-based photocatalysts in recent years were summarized in Table S3. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that the introduced CDs formed a heterojunction with HCNS, and an electric field was generated at the interface of the two materials to increase the separation and transportation of photogenerated carriers, as well as to decrease the recombination of electron-hole pairs [45,47]. However, the excessive CDs distribution on the surface of HCNS-Cx will darken the color of photocatalysts and affect the effective absorption of light, thus reducing the photocatalytic HER of HCNS-Cx [33]. The results suggest that there exists an optimum CDs content for CDs modified HCNS. In our experiment, the appropriate content of CDs is concurrent calcination of 10 g of cyanamide and 1 mg of glucose.

In order to investigate the influence of HCNS-C1.0 content on the water splitting activity, various amounts of HCNS-C1.0 (25 mg, 50 mg and 100 mg) were used to determine the optimal amount of catalyst under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). As presented in Fig. S5, the HER of 25, 50 and 100 mg HCNS-C1.0 were 2812, 2382, 1663 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively. The addition of high concentrations of HCNS-C1.0 would cause the masking effect on light scattering to limit the penetration of light through the solution, leading to the decrease the HER of HCNS-C1.0 [65]. Therefore, the highest HER of HCNS-C1.0 was obtained when the amount of HCNS-C1.0 was 25 mg.

To estimate the stability of the HCNS-C1.0, the cycling tests were carried out (Fig. 5c). After four cycles of repeated hydrogen production under the same conditions (λ > 420 nm), the photocatalytic performance was not decreased significantly, showing that HCNS-C1.0 has high cycling stability and cannot be easily photo corroded in the photocatalytic H2-evolution process. In addition, as shown in Figs. S6–S8, TEM images, XRD patterns and FTIR spectra of HCNS-C1.0 before and after the cycling tests were compared. The results showed that the morphology, structure and composition of HCNS-C1.0 had no obvious change, further demonstrating the excellent stability of HCNS-C1.0.

Furthermore, the photocurrent response and EIS are utilized to indicate the charge transportation behavior in the photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 5d, HCNS showed much stronger photocurrent responses than that of BCN, confirming the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs in HCNS. After CDs were introduced into HCNS, the photocurrent responses of HCNS-C1.0 was further enhanced. This result suggests that under the visible light irradiation, more photogenerated electrons were produced and transferred from HCNS to CDs, and then to electrodes [66]. Meanwhile, through the interface interaction between CDs and HNCS, the interfacial charge transfer resistance could be reduced, which is beneficial to the generation of active species and improve the migration efficiency of charge carriers. Thus, the hollow structures and the introduction of CDs could accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transport in HCNS-C1.0, thus significantly improving the H2 evolution.

In order to study the photocatalytic mechanism, the flat band potentials of BCN, HCNS and HCNS–C1.0 were calculated to be about −1.20 V, −1.29 V, and −1.08 V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. S9), respectively. As an n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (CB) position of g-C3N4-based materials is close to the flat band potential [67]. Therefore, the flat band potential can be used to substitute the CB potential to calculate the band-gap structures of the samples. Based on the results of band-gap and MS plots, the electronic band-gap structures of BCN, HCNS and HCNS-C1.0 are schematically illustrated in Fig. 5f. It can be found that whether constructing the hollow structures or introducing the CDs, the Valence band (VB) positions of as-prepared samples are kept almost constant, while the significant shifts are observed in CB position. For HCNS-C1.0, upon the intimate contact of CDs and HCNS, there is a Schottky barrier that allows electrons to transfer from HCNS to CDs. The accumulation of electrons will cause the CB potential of HCNS-C1.0 down-shifted to a more positive potential, thereby making the migration path of photogenerated electrons shorter [53]. Thus, the improvement of band-gap structure for HCNS-C1.0 is helpful to enhance the photocatalytic performance.

On this basis, the proposed mechanism of photocatalytic H2 evolution by HCNS-C1.0 is proposed in Fig. 6 . During the reaction of photocatalytic H2 evolution, CDs played an important role in improving the HER with HCNS-C1.0. On the one hand, the CDs served as photosensitizer could extend the light absorption range of HCNS-C1.0, leading to more photogenerated charge carriers. On the other hand, CDs could act as an electron transport medium, which can transfer the electrons produced by HCNS-C1.0 to Pt to participate in the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction. In this process, electron-hole pairs recombination rate was reduced significantly [66]. At the same time, the hollow structure can improve the visible light-harvesting by multiple reflection and scattering in the hollow cavity, hence, more photogenerated electron-hole pairs are generated. Therefore, the electrons could be excited from VB of HCNS-C1.0 to its CB by visible light, leaving the holes in its VB. The work function of cocatalyst Pt is 5.64 eV versus Evacuum (0.49 eV vs AgCl), which is positive than the CB of HCNS-C1.0 (−1.08 eV vs AgCl) [68]. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons are transferred to CDs loaded on HCNS. Then, electrons were further captured by cocatalyst Pt and reduced hydrogen ions (H+) to form H2, whereas the holes in the VB react with sacrificial reagents. Compared with BCN, on the one hand, the hollow structure of HCNS-C1.0 could increase its surface area, leading to create more reactive active sites. On the other hand, the introduction of CDs could significantly suppress the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs and increase the density of photogenerated carriers. Therefore, the sufficient reactive active sites and effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs may help to reduce H+ to form H2 on the surface of HCNS-C1.0.

In summary, a series of CDs modified hollow g-C3N4 spheres were constructed by one-step in situ thermal polymerization of two precursors cyanamide and glucose. The more efficient absorption of visible light, larger specific surface area and faster separation of electron-hole pairs were realized. Compared with BCN and HCNS, HCNS-Cx showed satisfactory photocatalytic performance for H2 evolution. Among them, the HER of HCNS-C1.0 (2322 μmol g−1 h−1) is 19 times higher than that of BCN (122 μmol g−1 h−1) and 1.8 times that of HCNS without CDs modification (1289 μmol g−1 h−1), respectively. And its AQE is 17.93% at 420 nm. This interesting result affords us a facile pathway to prepare the CDs/g-C3N4-based materials via one-step in situ approach.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2004173, 21274133) and the Open Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices (No. 2020-skllmd-08).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zou Y, Shi J, Ma D, Fan Z, Lu L, Niu C. In situ synthesis of C-doped TiO2@g-C3N4 core-shell hollow nanospheres with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution. Chem Eng J 2017; 322: 435-444.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ag-doped TiO2 photocatalysts with effective charge transfer for highly efficient hydrogen production through water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gogoi D, Namdeo A, Golder A K, Peela N R. Ag-doped TiO2 photocatalysts with effective charge transfer for highly efficient hydrogen production through water splitting. Int J Hydrogen Energy 2020; 45: 2729-2744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of carbon quantum dot modified 1D protonated nanorods of graphitic carbon nitride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li L, Zhu X. Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of carbon quantum dot modified 1D protonated nanorods of graphitic carbon nitride. ACS Appl Nano Mater 2018; 1: 5337-5344.
